coredata.dc:title: Manipulating singlet oxygen generation on Co nanoclusters-confined g-C3N4 macroscopic beads for boosted water decontamination
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Qiao, Yiyang
Yu, Ping
Zhang, Yingying
Zhou, Yan
Wan, Yuntian
Dai, Jiangdong
coredata.dc:description: Reducing the migration distance of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and enhancing interfacial electron transfer represent effective approaches for enhancing the reactivity of heterogeneous catalysts, albeit still posing significant challenges. Herein, we successfully synthesized ultrasmall cobalt nanoclusters-confined g-C3N4 macroscopic beads with N-doped carbon dots (Co/NC@CN beads). Compared to unconfined Co/C and Co/C@CN beads, Co/NC@CN beads demonstrated remarkable utilization efficiency of PMS (84.3%), achieving complete degradation of TC within 20 min at a rate of 162.1 min-1·M-1, surpassing most reported catalysts. Besides, Co/NC@CN beads predominantly generated singlet oxygen (1O2), ensuring efficient removal of micropollutants with resistance to pH and complex water bodies. Experiments have proven that the strong interaction between Co clusters and g-C3N4 beads containing NC dots facilitated the generation of interfacial electron transfer by optimizing the electronic structure of Co nanoclusters, thereby Co/NC@CN beads could capture electrons from tetracycline (TC) and PMS molecules towards dissolved oxygen (DO) to form O2·-, which subsequently converted into 1O2. More importantly, the efficiency of the flow-through unit in the continuous degradation of TC with zero discharge was substantiated by long-term experiments, and its performance could be restored through a straightforward low-temperature carbonization process. This study offers a universal design framework for the creation of various confined macroscopic beads, enabling the achievement of highly effective wastewater treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Macroscopic Beads
Confinement
Strong interaction
Oxygen vacancy
Singlet oxygen

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Possible catalytic mechanism of Co-O/CN beads

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM, HETEM images of (b) Co/C beads, (c) Co/NC beads, (d) Co/NC@CN powder, (e) Co/C@CN beads, and (f) Co/NC@CN beads, and (g) EDS mapping images of Co/NC@CN beads.
(a) XRD patterns of different catalysts, (b) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, (c) and (d) pore size distribution of different samples.

XPS spectra of (a) Co 2p, (b) O 1 s, (d) N 1 s, and (e) C 1 s of different samples, and (c) low-temperature EPR and (f) EIS spectra of Co/C beads, Co/NC beads, Co/C@CN beads, Co/NC@CN powder, and Co/NC@CN beads.

(a) Degradation efficiencies with various systems, (b) comparison of normalized kinetics of organic pollutant degradation and the literature reports, (c) PMS consumption, (d) degradation efficiencies in various water environments, and (e) different pollutants.

The (a) reusability and (b) fluidized-column catalytic unit fluidized-column catalytic unit of the Co/NC@CN beads/PMS system, (c) degradation pathways of and (d) 3D fluorescence spectra of TC with 0 min, 10 min, and 20 min in the Co/NC@CN beads.

EPR spectra of (a) SO4·- and ·OH, (b) O2·-, and (c) 1O2, (d) comparison of removal efficiencies of TC with different quenchers, (e) steady-state concentrations of ROS, and (f) removal of TC under air, N2 and O2 atmospheres.

(a) I-t curves monitoring upon sequential injection of PMS and TC, (b) LSV curves of Co/NC@CN beads, (c) In-situ Raman spectra, and (d) In-situ FTIR spectra, catalysts, and XPS spectra of (e) Co 2p and (f) O 1 s before and after reaction.
Proposed possible mechanism of PMS activation by Co/NC@CN beads toward TC degradation.

head.ce:title: Manipulating singlet oxygen generation on Co nanoclusters-confined g-C3N4 macroscopic beads for boosted water decontamination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Computer Science, Jilin Normal University, 1301 Haifeng Street, Siping 136000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Computer Science, Jilin Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Computer Science, Jilin Normal University, 1301 Haifeng Street, Siping 136000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reducing the migration distance of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and enhancing interfacial electron transfer represent effective approaches for enhancing the reactivity of heterogeneous catalysts, albeit still posing significant challenges. Herein, we successfully synthesized ultrasmall cobalt nanoclusters-confined g-C3N4 macroscopic beads with N-doped carbon dots (Co/NC@CN beads). Compared to unconfined Co/C and Co/C@CN beads, Co/NC@CN beads demonstrated remarkable utilization efficiency of PMS (84.3%), achieving complete degradation of TC within 20 min at a rate of 162.1 min-1·M-1, surpassing most reported catalysts. Besides, Co/NC@CN beads predominantly generated singlet oxygen (1O2), ensuring efficient removal of micropollutants with resistance to pH and complex water bodies. Experiments have proven that the strong interaction between Co clusters and g-C3N4 beads containing NC dots facilitated the generation of interfacial electron transfer by optimizing the electronic structure of Co nanoclusters, thereby Co/NC@CN beads could capture electrons from tetracycline (TC) and PMS molecules towards dissolved oxygen (DO) to form O2·-, which subsequently converted into 1O2. More importantly, the efficiency of the flow-through unit in the continuous degradation of TC with zero discharge was substantiated by long-term experiments, and its performance could be restored through a straightforward low-temperature carbonization process. This study offers a universal design framework for the creation of various confined macroscopic beads, enabling the achievement of highly effective wastewater treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Peroxymonosulfate (PMS)-based advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) offer a promising clean technology for generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) to address the challenges posed by organic pollutants containing benzene ring structures in natural water systems [1,2]. The activation of PMS using transition metals (TMs) and their oxides has been widely employed to generate ROS and facilitate the degradation of persistent pollutants [3,4]. However, the activation of heterogeneous PMS still faces limitations in effectively converting the oxidation state to the reduction state [5–7]. Additionally, the leaching of metal ions and excessive energy and resource consumption present significant challenges that negatively impact catalytic stability. These challenges act as bottlenecks, hindering the progress of PMS-AOP systems [8]. To optimize the reactivity and durability of TM-based catalysts, it is crucial to thoroughly consider strategies that can be employed by the PMS-advanced oxidation process system to achieve this objective.

To mitigate the inherent limitations of catalysts based on transition metals (TMs), the containment of impurities within nano-scale reactors (channels, interfaces, pores, cavities, nanocages, membrane, etc.) frequently demonstrates remarkable physical and chemical characteristics often exhibits unprecedented physical and chemical properties [9]. The behavior of matter in a confined environment differs from that in the bulk phase, exerting a significant influence on oxidation reactions [10]. Recently, the phenomenon of strong metal-support interaction has emerged as a means to not only effectively modulate the electronic structure of metals and enhance their stability through chemical confinement, but also to amplify the concentration of defects, thereby further enhancing catalytic activity [11,12]. Specifically, the presence of oxygen vacancies (OV) has been shown to influence the electrical characteristics of metal-based catalysts and create active sites with an abundance of electrons [13]. Upon formation of an OV, electrons in close proximity to the vacancy tend to occupy the O 2p orbital before becoming delocalized, thereby enhancing the electron transfer capabilities of the catalysts [14]. Consequently, there exists a compelling impetus to develop a catalyst featuring a confined structure that induces a discrepancy in surface polarization, thereby facilitating the directed flow of electrons and offering appropriate adsorption sites for the effective utilization of PMS.

The use of heterojunctions/interfaces is commonly employed in Fenton/Fenton-like catalysis due to their capacity to regulate the electronic structure through interconnected interfaces [15]. The confinement of TM-based catalysts within a graphite carbon layer has been demonstrated to modify their electron density [16,17]. This modification serves as an electronic bridge, decreasing the local work function of TM and increasing the generation of ROS. Composite materials display a variety of characteristics inherent to their constituent compounds as a result of the coupling effect stemming from metal-carbon interactions [18,19]. The reactivity of graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) or carbon nanotubes-supported transition metal (TM) nanoparticles (NPs) in the activation of peroxymonosulfate (PMS) has been verified in previous studies [20–22]. Recent studies found that nitrogen-containing surfactants such as pyrrolidone (PVP) as capping or linking agents can increase the repulsive force between the metal particles, promoting large nanoparticles to further convert ultrafine nanocluster during the carbonization process [23,24]. However, to the best of our knowledge, it has rarely been reported that vacancy-rich cobalt nanoclusters-confined g-C3N4 macroscopic beads are used for efficient wastewater treatment.

Based on the aforementioned findings, the present study utilized a combination of coordination crosslinking and carbothermal reduction techniques to produce a unique catalyst with an interfacial confined microenvironment, namely Co/NC@CN beads. These beads were fabricated using Co(II)-g-C3N4/sodium alginate beads as the precursor with PVP as a capping agent to modulate the nanoparticles' size. The Co/NC@CN beads were employed for the effective activation of PMS to degrade TC. A comprehensive investigation was conducted to assess the influence of various variables on the degradation of TC. Additionally, the cyclability and long-term stability of the Co/NC@CN beads were examined in a flow-through unit. Overall, this work provides new insights into the efficient and recyclable synthesis of macroscopic catalysts for large-scale wastewater treatment.

To initiate the production of Co/NC@CN macroscopic beads, 650 mg of g-C3N4 was dispersed in 100 mL of deionized water. This dispersion process lasted for 6.0 h, utilizing a cell pulverizer operating at a power level of 540 W. Subsequently, 1200 mg of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and 1200 mg of sodium alginate (SA) were added to the solution. The resulting mixture was vigorously stirred for 6.0 h, followed by a 0.5 h sonication treatment. To facilitate the chelation process and create Co(II)-PVP/SA/CN hydrogel beads, the initial mixture was introduced into a 500 mL solution of Co(NO3)2·6 H2O in 75% ethanol solution with a concentration of 0.2 mol/L. This addition was carried out using a peristaltic pump at a consistent speed. After a 12 h cross-linking period, excess cobalt ions were removed from the hydrogel beads using deionized water. For comparison purposes, Co(II)/CN powder was synthesized without SA, and Co(II)/SA catalyst was synthesized without g-C3N4. The dried hydrogel beads were carbonized at 300 °C for 2.0 h with a constant heating rate of 5.0° C/min. Finally, the resulting products were labeled as Co/NC@CN beads. In contrast, Co/NC beads with PVP as nitrogen sources and Co/C beads without g-C3N4 and PVP were fabricated, respectively, while the production of Co/NC@CN powder did not involve SA as a cross-linking agent.

A series of reactions were conducted at room temperature to evaluate the degradation efficiency of TC as the targeted contaminant. Typically, 5.0 mg of Co/NC@CN beads and 50 mL of TC solution (20 mg/L) were added to 100 mL beaker and continuously stirred for 30 min to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Following this, specific amounts of PMS were introduced into the system to initiate the reaction. Samples of 2.0 mL were collected at predetermined time intervals after filtration and promptly mixed with 2.0 mL of methanol as a quencher for subsequent analysis. Following the reaction, the Co/NC@CN beads were gathered and subjected to thorough washing to prepare them for subsequent investigations regarding their potential for reusability. Furthermore, to evaluate the catalytic performance in a dynamic setting, a quantity of 30 mg of Co/NC@CN beads was introduced into a column reactor. Subsequently, a solution containing a mixture of PMS (500 mg/L) and TC (10 mg/L) was introduced into the reactor at various flow rates utilizing a peristaltic pump. At predetermined time intervals, samples were extracted from the solution, combined with methanol, and subjected to testing to ascertain the residual total concentration.

This study presented the successful fabrication of macroscopic beads, as illustrated in Fig. S1, through a comprehensive step-by-step process. We employed a metal-ion-induced self-assembly strategy to facilitate the growth of ultra-small cobalt nanoparticles on SA@CN through electrostatic attraction and coordination interactions [25]. The gradual decomposition of organic units during the pyrolysis process hindered the crystallization of the nanoparticles at a lower temperature. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images depicted in Fig. 1a and S2 demonstrated that the Co/NC@CN beads consist of numerous two-dimensional nanosheets. This aligns with our expectations, as the inherent characteristics of conventional 2D lamellar structures offer significant advantages in terms of increased surface area and exposure to active sites. Moreover, the presence of a carbon network within these structures effectively facilitates charge and electron transfer [26,27]. It was observed that g-C3N4 had a typical 2D nanosheet structure (Fig. S3), while large cobalt nanoparticles were found in the Co/C beads without PVP as a capping agent (Fig. 1b), while the Co/NC beads had a typical 2D structure in the presence of PVP as nitrogen sources (Fig. 1c), which confirmed that PVP could modulate the size of metal particles. However, obvious Co-based nanoparticles were also found in the Co/NC@CN powder and Co/C@CN beads (Figs. 1d and 1e), we guessed that this result may be attributed to the low coordinated ability between g-C3N4 and Co2+ [28]. Fig. 1f reveals the sheet-like porous morphology of the Co/NC@CN beads, while the HRTEM image of Co/NC@CN beads did not show the obvious lattice of cobalt species, which confirmed the uniform distribution of cobalt nanoclusters due to the strong metal-supporting interaction. The preparation of Co2+-PVP/SA/CN hydrogel beads was accomplished using the sol-gel method, without the removal of PVP. This resulted in the creation of a restricted area between the SA template and g-C3N4 nanosheets effectively inhibiting the growth of metal species. The Co precursor underwent decomposition in the confined space between the SA template and g-C3N4 nanosheets. After undergoing high-temperature carbonization, the Co nanoclusters were effectively confined within g-C3N4 beads that contained NC dots, resulting in a highly dispersed distribution. Analysis using energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) of Co/NC@CN beads (Fig. 1g) demonstrated a uniform distribution of Co, C, N, and O elements, confirming the successful fabrication of confined Co/NC@CN beads.

As shown in Fig. 2a, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of Co/NC@CN beads, exhibited two distinct peaks at 13.1° and 26.7°, corresponding to the (100) and (002) planes of the g-C3N4 substrate. This observation confirmed that Co/NC@CN beads maintained the interlayer layering structure of the g-C3N4 substrate. On the contrary, for Co/C beads, three diffraction peaks located at 36.8°, 59.3°, and 65.2°, corresponding to (222), (511), and (440) planes, respectively (Co3O4 (JCPDS NO.43–1003), which further verified the role of PVP [29]. The specific surface area and porous volume of the samples were determined using the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm (Figs. 2b-2d, S4, and Table S1). The observed isotherm for all samples exhibited a typical II-type, indicating the presence of hierarchical mesoporous structures. These architectures have been proven to enhance the permeation of reactants towards active catalytic sites, thereby increasing the accessibility of active sites for the sequestration of specific contaminants [30]. Moreover, the Co/NC@CN beads demonstrated a favorable specific surface area (58.55 m2/g) and pore volume (0.31 cm3/g). These values were significantly higher compared to the Co/NC beads (8.49 m2/g and 0.03 cm3/g) and Co/C beads (8.12 m2/g and 0.07 cm3/g), indicating that the incorporation of g-C3N4 was more advantageous in terms of exposing active sites. More significantly, the successful implementation of pore engineering was demonstrated by the modulation of pore diameters from 2.82 to 1.50 nm, leading to an increased number of catalytically active sites for PMS molecules and improved transfer of reactants and products through interconnected channels.

To investigate the chemical compositions and chemical states of the catalysts, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed (Table S2). The obtained results, as depicted in Fig. 3a, revealed prominent peaks at 781.4 and 797.2 eV, which corresponded to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, respectively. These peaks indicated the presence of Co3+ species. Notably, when compared to Co-O/C beads, the incorporation of g-C3N4 resulted in a smaller shift of the Co 2p binding energy, suggesting a higher proportion of Co3+ species. Notably, the Co 2p spectra of Co/NC@CN powder were less pronounced, which can be attributed to the stronger coordination between SA and Co2+ compared to the adsorption of g-C3N4 onto Co2+. In Fig. 3b, the O 1 s spectra were deconvoluted into three peaks: hydroxyl groups or surface-adsorbed oxygen species (OS, 532.7–533.6 eV), lattice oxygen (OL, 530.2–531.3 eV), and oxygen vacancy (OV, 531.9–532.3 eV). Interestingly, the ratio of OV/OL in Co/NC@CN beads was 1.79, significantly higher than those of Co/NC (0.71) and Co/C (1.24) beads, indicating that Co/NC@CN beads had a greater potential for PMS activation [31]. Additionally, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were employed to explore the content of oxygen vacancies in the catalysts. The characteristic peak intensity of OV in Co/NC@CN beads was stronger than those of Co/NC@CN powder and Co/NC beads, consistent with the results of XPS analysis, which suggested that strong metal-supporting interaction could increase the concentration of OV (Fig. 3c). The presence of peaks at Co-O-C/Co-N in the Co/NC@CN beads, in contrast to the Co/C beads and Co/NC@CN powder, suggested that the combination of g-C3N4 and PVP promoted the formation of more Co-O-C/Co-N bonds (Figs. 3d and 3e) [32]. Conversely, the absence of signals for the N element in the Co/C beads further indicates that the presence of PVP significantly affected the formation of NC dots. The noticeable changes in the C 1 s and N 1 s spectra provided evidence for the enhanced electronic structures of the Co/NC@CN beads resulting from the strong metal-supporting interaction effect. Furthermore, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to verify the charge transfer efficiency on the catalyst surface. As expected, Co/NC@CN beads exhibited the smallest half-circle radius, indicating more efficient charge transfer at the interface. We speculated that this enhanced charge transfer efficiency may be attributed to the presence of Co-O-C/Co-N bonding bridges, which greatly enhance electron transfer efficiency (Fig. 3f). The Co nanoclusters were effectively synthesized using a physical confinement approach, as evidenced by the characterization results. Furthermore, the presence of g-C3N4 beads containing NC dots facilitated a robust metal-supporting interaction, leading to enhanced electronic structures of Co centers.

The catalytic activity of Co/NC@CN beads in activating PMS with TC as the targeted pollutant was thoroughly evaluated in this study. As depicted in Fig. 4a and S5a, pure PMS was able to remove approximately 15% of TC in the absence of catalysts. The adsorption of catalysts onto TC was negligible before the addition of PMS, indicating that the removal of TC was primarily driven by the catalytic process, while the Co/C beads/PMS, Co/NC beads/PMS, Co/C@CN beads/PMS, and Co/NC@CN powder/PMS systems could remove 39.3, 97.3, 54.3, and 90.4% respectively within 30 min, which implied the confined microenvironment was more favorable for interfacial electron transfer to promote PMS activation. Post-reaction Co ion leaching across various systems exhibited a positive correlation with their reactivity (Fig. S5b), and the contribution of homogeneous catalysis by Co ions at equivalent concentrations to TC degradation was trivial when juxtaposed with the swift heterogeneous degradation observed (Fig. S5c). Furthermore, the degradation rate of the Co/NC@CN beads/PMS system (0.198 min-1) in TC removal was 19.4, 1.83, and 11.37 times higher than those of the Co/C beads, Co/NC beads and Co/NC@CN powder, respectively. Notably, the Co/NC@CN beads exhibited a normalized k-value of 162.1 min-1·M-1 in TC degradation, which significantly surpassed the majority of previously reported studies (Fig. 4b, S5a, and Table S3). Furthermore, the optimization of catalyst dosage, TC concentration, and PMS concentration was conducted (Fig. S5d-S5f). Notably, the Co/NC@CN beads demonstrated the highest PMS utilization efficiency (84.3%), followed by Co/C beads (32.5%), Co/NC beads (67.4%), Co/NC@CN powder (72.5%), and Co/C@CN beads (48.1%), which suggested that the presence of Co-O-C/Co-N bonding bridges greatly enhance the electron transfer between catalysts and PMS molecules via a confined space (Fig. 4c). The investigation also investigated the examination of the initial pH value, environmental background ions, and reaction temperature (Fig. S6). The Co/NC@CN beads/PMS system exhibited remarkable resistance to interference within a broad pH range of 4–10 and displayed an activation energy of 29.4 kJ/mol, significantly lower than that of most catalysts reported in the literature [22,33–35]. Moreover, the Co-O/CN beads demonstrated exceptional performance across different water conditions, as depicted in Fig. 4d. Even when tested in the effluent of Yudai River (Jiangsu University, China) and Sea River (Hong Kong), it achieved a TC removal efficiency of over 85%. The catalytic efficacy of the Co/NC@CN beads/PMS system was further investigated for the degradation of various organic pollutants, including levofloxacin (LVX), ciprofloxacin (CIP), methylene blue (MB), rhodamine B (RhB), and phenol. As shown in Fig. 4e, RhB, MB, CIP, and phenol were nearly completely decomposed within a mere 12 min, highlighting the remarkable versatility of the Co/NC@CN beads/PMS system.

The stability and reusability of Co/NC@CN beads were comprehensively examined. As depicted in Fig. 5a, the degradation efficiency of TC remained consistently high at approximately 90% even after undergoing four cycles. To restore the degradation performance, the samples underwent carbonization at a temperature of 200 °C for a duration of 2.0 h, still achieving more than 90% TC removal. This enhancement can be attributed to the carbonization of the adsorption of generated intermediates on the catalysts, thereby effectively promoting the exposure of active sites. To evaluate the enduring stability of Co/NC@CN beads, a fluidized-column reactor was utilized, resulting in the successful removal of more than 95% TC with a total volume of 500 mL and negligible Co ion leaching (GB 25467–2010 and USEPA’s secondary drinking water standard, 1.0 mg/L) (Fig. 5b and S7) [36,37]. As expected, the formation of a physically confined microenvironment in the Co/NC@CN beads, featuring Co-O-C/Co-N binding electron bridges, exhibited an excellent interfacial electron transfer process. These findings revealed the favorable prospects of employing Co/NC@CN beads in real-world applications for sewage treatment. As depicted in Fig. 5c, S8, and Table S4, A plausible degradation pathway of TC in the Co/NC@CN beads/PMS system was proposed by using LC-MS analysis to investigate the produced intermediates. The peak of interest exhibited a significant correlation with an m/z value of 445, subsequently undergoing conversion into several intermediate compounds. The majority of intermediate species displayed initial enhancement followed by a decrease in peak intensity as the reaction time progressed. This observation suggested that these intermediates undergo fragmentation into smaller molecules and undergo further oxidation, ultimately forming CO2 and H2O. This result aligned with the intensity of the characteristic peaks observed in the 3D excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectroscopy of TC and its intermediates (Fig. 5d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible catalytic mechanism of Co-O/CN beads
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the primary ROS involved, the utilization of EPR spectroscopy was employed to investigate the presence of both radicals and non-radicals. This investigation employed 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidinyl (TEMP) as trapping agents [38]. As illustrated in Fig. 6a, the EPR characteristic signals corresponding to DMPO-SO4·- and DMPO-·OH were observed, providing evidence for the formation of SO4·- and ·OH species. It was worth noting that the signal intensity of DMPO-SO4·- and ·OH remained relatively stable after 10 min, indicating the significant involvement of SO4·- and ·OH in the degradation of TC. Conversely, the signal intensity of DMPO-O2·- exhibited a noticeable decrease in the Co/NC@CN beads/PMS system after 10 min, suggesting that O2·- played a crucial role in degradation of TC (Fig. 6b). However, considering the low redox potential of O2·- (-0.33 V vs NHE), its effectiveness in degrading pollutants is limited, and it is generally regarded as an essential intermediate for 1O2 [39]. In Fig. 6c, the presence of the TEMP-1O2 adduct in the Co/NC@CN beads/PMS system was observed to increase gradually with the duration of the reaction, suggesting the continuous involvement of 1O2 in the degradation of TC over Co/NC@CN beads. Importantly, compared to unconfined Co/C beads/PMS, and Co/C@CN beads/PMS systems, the stronger characteristic signal of TEMP-1O2 was observed in the Co/NC@CN beads/PMS system (Fig. S9), which highlighted more and faster electron transfer through confined effect to selectively generate abundant 1O2. Furthermore, the measurements conducted under N2 aeration to eliminate dissolved oxygen (DO) in the solution resulted in a significant reduction in the signals of DMPO-O2·- and TEMP-1O2. Conversely, the stronger characteristic signal observed in the Co/NC@CN beads/PMS system indicated a higher concentration of O2·- and 1O2 compared to other reaction systems under saturated O2 conditions. This finding suggested that the Co/NC@CN beads with unique confined spaces facilitated the efficient transfer of electrons, leading to the rapid activation of PMS and the generation of abundant free radicals. Furthermore, the addition of p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) substantially suppressed the signal of TEMP-1O2, further supporting the conversion of O2·- to 1O2 [35].

To further confirm these assumptions, we conducted chemical quenching experiments employing methanol (MeOH), tert-butanol (TBA), p-BQ, and furfuryl alcohol (FFA) (Fig. 6d) [40]. The inclusion of p-BQ and FFA resulted in significant inhibition of removal efficiencies in all systems, indicating that 1O2 played a dominant role in the degradation of TC over Co/NC@CN beads. Conversely, the addition of MeOH and TBA only resulted in slight inhibition in the Co/NC@CN beads/PMS system, suggesting that SO4·- and ·OH had a minor impact on the Co/NC@CN beads/PMS system. Notably, the inhibition results with the addition of FFA in the Co/C beads/PMS and the Co/C@CN powder/PMS systems were lower compared to that of the Co/NC@CN beads/PMS system, which indicated that the interfacial confined microenvironment could modulate the selective generation of ROS (Fig. S10).

The concentrations of reactive species [·OH, SO4·-, O2·-, and 1O2] under steady-state conditions were determined using benzoic acid (BA), nitrobenzene (NB), Nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT), and 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) as probes, respectively. The concentrations of the probe compounds were significantly lower than that of the target micropollutant TC, ensuring precise quantification. Among the reactive oxidation species, 1O2 was determined to be the dominant species in the Co/NC@CN beads/PMS system, with concentrations significantly higher than those of SO4·- and ·OH, by factors of three to 4.1 and 13.3, respectively (Fig. 6e). This observation suggested that the Co/NC@CN beads/PMS system produces elevated levels of O2·- and 1O2. Additionally, the impact of dissolved oxygen on the reaction was examined by purging the system with saturated N2. The degradation efficiency of TC was significantly reduced to 52.5% (Fig. 6f), suggesting that the activation of PMS by Co/NC@CN beads involved the reduction of O2 to O2·- and subsequent conversion to 1O2, which played a crucial role.

The electrochemical analysis findings offer conclusive evidence that supports the existence of electronic interaction between PMS and catalysts. The amperometric i-t curves of Co/NC@CN beads demonstrated a noteworthy surge in current upon the introduction of PMS (ΔI+PMS), gradually stabilizing at an equilibrium value. Concurrently, the redox potential rose significantly with the subsequent addition of TC ( Fig. 7a). This observation implied that the interaction between PMS and the catalyst leads to the formation of a metastable intermediate known as catalyst-PMS*, resulting in an increase in the oxidation potential of the catalyst by facilitating partial electron transfer from the catalyst to PMS [41,42].

Following this, the catalyst-PMS* complexes proceed to extract an electron from the adsorbed organics via Co-O-C/C-N bonds, thereby adopting a galvanic-like cell mechanism [43]. Additionally, the linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) profiles depicted in Fig. 7b demonstrated a rise in current for the Co/NC@CN beads electrode following the introduction of PMS into the electrolyte. This augmentation signified the transfer of electrons from PMS to Co/NC@CN beads. Moreover, the concurrent presence of TC and PMS led to a further increase in current, corroborating the findings of the previous i-t analysis. This observation implied that TC and PMS could individually bind to active sites, impeding the transportation of electrons from reactants to Co/NC@CN beads. Additionally, the enhanced intensity of the Co/NC@CN beads, compared to Co/C beads, Co/NC beads, Co/C@CN beads, and Co/NC@CN powder, indicated that the Co/NC@CN beads facilitated mass transfer more effectively (Fig. S11).

To investigate the phenomenon of interfacial electron transfer, we conducted in-situ Raman and FTIR spectra. The analysis of the pure PMS revealed the presence of three prominent peaks at 880, 970, and 1048 cm-1, which corresponded to the stretching vibrations of O-O, SO4 2-, and SO3 - (Fig. 7c). Upon interaction with PMS, the intensities of the O-O and SO3 - peaks exhibited a noticeable decrease in Co/NC@CN beads, indicating the activation of PMS over these beads. Additionally, the SO3 - peak underwent a shift from 1048 to 1067 cm-1, suggesting the occurrence of electron transfer between PMS and Co-O/CN beads, which was consistent with the findings of LSV analysis. Moreover, the I970/I1048 ratio, which represented the peak intensity ratio at 970 cm-1 to that at 1048 cm-1, has been established as a reliable indicator of PMS consumption. A higher I970/I1048 value signified a greater extent of PMS consumption [44]. In comparison to PMS alone, the interaction between Co/NC@CN beads and PMS resulted in an increased I970/I1048 ratio, providing further evidence of PMS activation by Co/NC@CN beads. Conversely, the addition of TC to the Co/NC@CN beads and PMS suspension led to a decrease in this ratio, indicating that TC facilitated the accumulation of PMS consumption over Co/NC@CN beads. These findings align with the results obtained from electrochemical tests and PMS decomposition experiments. These findings indicated that the degradation of TC over Co/NC@CN beads was facilitated by a self-catalytic oxidation process involving dual-active centers mediated by PMS. This observation was supported by similar results obtained from in situ FTIR spectra (Fig. 7d). Additionally, slight changes in the XPS survey spectra of Co/NC@CN beads before and after reaction were observed (Fig. S12a). Notably, the peaks of Co 2p in Fig. 7e exhibited a shift of 0.3 eV towards higher binding energy, suggesting an enhanced oxidation state of Co species [45]. The O 1 s spectra shifted to lower energy after reacting with PMS (Fig. 7f) and the N 1 s and C 1 s spectra exhibited an obvious change (Fig. S12b and S12c), suggesting the self-oxidation cycle of Co/NC@CN beads with Co-N/Co-O-C bonds as electron bridges. Furthermore, the EPR signal of Co/NC@CN beads was observed both before and after reaction, providing further confirmation of these findings (Fig. S11d).

A plausible catalytic mechanism was proposed in Fig. 8, based on the obtained results. Initially, the Co/NC@CN beads, which had a highly positive-charged surface, demonstrated rapid attraction and enrichment of negatively charged TC and PMS on the catalyst surface. Subsequently, the Co-O-C/Co-N bonds acted as an electron bridge, facilitating the electron transfer from TC to PMS due to the significant polarity disparity in an interfacial confined microenvironment. More specifically, TC donated electrons to the electron-deficient Co center, which were then transferred to the electron-rich OV center as a result of the polarity contrast. Similarly, PMS adsorbed onto the surface and captured these electrons at the OV center, leading to the generation of ROS for TC degradation.

In summary, we have effectively successfully synthesized ultrasmall cobalt nanoclusters-confined g-C3N4 macroscopic beads with N-doped carbon dots. These beads possessed dual-reaction centers that facilitated the efficient activation of PMS via an interfacial confined microenvironment. Empirical evidence substantiated that the Co/NC@CN beads exhibited exceptional efficacy and enduring stability when employed for the treatment of large-scale wastewater. Experiments have proven that the strong interaction between Co clusters and g-C3N4 beads containing NC dots facilitated the generation of interfacial electron transfer by optimizing the electronic structure of Co nanoclusters, thereby Co/NC@CN beads could capture electrons from tetracycline (TC) and PMS molecules towards dissolved oxygen (DO) to form O2·- which subsequently converted into 1O2. Consequently, this investigation has shed light on the mechanism underlying the production of ROS via the interaction between electron-rich and electron-poor reaction sites, offering a practical strategy for the design and optimization of metal-based catalysts to effectively remediate natural water sources.

Yiyang Qiao: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ping Yu: Writing – original draft, Resources, Project administration, Investigation. Yingying Zhang: Software, Methodology, Investigation. Yan Zhou: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yuntian Wan: Software, Investigation, Data curation. Jiangdong Dai: Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22306075), the Postdoctoral Research Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 2022M721382 and 2022M711402), and Jiangsu Funding Program for Excellent Postdoctoral Talent (2023ZB108 and 2023ZB453).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.134376.
